articles.title: Canada: Government audit finds impact of international student cap far greater than expected
articles.summary: The Office of the Auditor General of Canada (OAG) released an independent audit report assessing the government’s reforms to Canada’s international student programme. The report found that the impact of the international student cap was significantly greater than anticipated.
articles.insight:
Canada’s unexpected over-cap on international students may indirectly heighten demand for alternatives in the US, UK, Australia, and New Zealand—though actual spillover depends on student awareness, eligibility, and perceived institutional fit, not just policy shifts.  
Admissions teams should track application trends from Canada’s top source countries (e.g., India, China, Philippines), monitor visa processing times and policy updates in those markets, and assess whether their outreach messaging resonates with students seeking stability amid global immigration uncertainty.

articles.title: Australia: Latest enrolment data challenges the government’s assertion of stability for international education this year
articles.summary: On 20 March, Australia’s Assistant Minister for International Education, Julian Hill, published a statement titled “Continuity and change: the year ahead for international education,” asserting that international education is a “national strength” and emphasizing policy stability as foundational to its evolution. However, the latest enrolment data contradicts the government’s claim of stability in the sector this year.
articles.insight:
Australia’s latest enrolment data—showing divergence from official claims of sectoral stability—may indicate softening demand, shifting source-market preferences, or early effects of policy changes, though causality remains unclear amid broader global mobility trends and economic pressures.  
Admissions teams should track Australia’s quarterly enrolment reports, visa grant rates by nationality, and competitor messaging from UK/US institutions to assess whether sentiment shifts are emerging in key markets like India, China, or Nigeria.

articles.title: “They are taking away the few opportunities left”: Chevening applicants react to UK visa brake
articles.summary: The UK’s student visa restrictions led to the abrupt termination of Chevening Scholarship applications, disproportionately affecting female Afghan students who are currently barred from higher education under Taliban rule. Shahira Sadat, an affected applicant, described the emotional impact of the policy and expressed diminished faith in the global community’s support.
articles.insight:
This news signals potential shifts in source market sentiment, particularly among Afghan and other high-risk region applicants, as UK visa tightening may erode trust in traditional study destinations—though broader demand impacts remain uncertain without data on actual application declines across other markets.  
Admissions teams should monitor inquiries and application trends from Afghanistan and neighbouring regions, while reviewing messaging around visa support and institutional commitments to inclusivity, especially where geopolitical instability intersects with education access restrictions.

articles.title: RMIT adds Bachelor of International Studies program for 'global citizens'
articles.summary: RMIT Vietnam launched its Bachelor of International Studies program in October. The program aims to train students to work effectively in international environments.
articles.insight:
RMIT Vietnam’s new Bachelor of International Studies signals growing regional appetite for globally oriented, locally delivered degrees—potentially reflecting shifting preferences among Southeast Asian students who may increasingly weigh cost, proximity, and employability over traditional Western study destinations, though direct impact on Anglophone universities remains uncertain.  
Admissions teams should monitor enrolment trends in similar interdisciplinary, globally focused programs across ASEAN campuses and track whether RMIT’s model spurs local competitors to expand—informing scholarship positioning and articulation pathway development with regional institutions.
